text: A woman sued a fast-food chain after spilling hot coffee on herself.
verdict: Real — she won. The coffee was dangerously hot. (Liebeck v. McDonald's, 1994)

text: A man sued a beer company claiming their adverts falsely promised him a fun social life.
verdict: Real — he lost. The court was not amused.

text: A burglar sued homeowners after getting stuck in their garage and living on dog food for days.
verdict: Real — and he actually won a settlement.

text: Someone sued themselves and asked the state to pay the damages.
verdict: Real — it happened.

text: A man tried to divorce his wife by delivering the papers via carrier pigeon.
verdict: Fake — carrier pigeons are not recognised process servers.

text: A company was sued because its 'magic diet tea' did not, in fact, teleport fat away.
verdict: Fake — no quantum weight-loss products have reached court.

text: A man sued God for the weather and asked for an injunction against storms.
verdict: Real — the case was dismissed for lack of service.

text: A prisoner sued himself for violating his own civil rights and demanded $5 million.
verdict: Real — he asked the state to pay since he was a ward of the state.

text: A woman sued a zoo after a peacock screamed at her and ruined her marriage proposal.
verdict: Fake — peacocks are dramatic, but not legally liable.

text: A tenant sued their landlord because the house was haunted and no ghosts were disclosed.
verdict: Fake — no material-ghost disclosure requirement yet.

text: A judge once ruled a burrito is not a sandwich.
verdict: Real — the famous 'what is a sandwich' case set food-law precedent.

text: A company trademarked the sound of a chainsaw starting.
verdict: Fake — though some sounds are trademarked, this one is not.

text: A man sued a dry cleaner for $67 million over a missing pair of trousers.
verdict: Real — he lost and the case became legendary.

text: A city council banned ice cream trucks after one gave a judge brain freeze.
verdict: Fake — judges have ruled through worse.

text: A woman sued Google Maps after she walked onto a motorway following its directions.
verdict: Real — she sued for being led into traffic.

text: A cat was called as a witness in a murder trial and had to 'swear' on a Bible.
verdict: Fake — cats make unreliable witnesses.

text: A student sued their university for not warning them that law school was hard.
verdict: Fake — but the feeling is real.

text: A court once had to decide whether a Jaffa Cake was a cake or a biscuit for tax purposes.
verdict: Real — it was ruled a cake, saving it from higher biscuit tax.

text: A lawyer objected to his own question in the middle of asking it.
verdict: Fake — but every litigator has wanted to.

text: A man sued his neighbour because their dog mocked him by copying his walk.
verdict: Fake — no legal remedy for canine impressions.

text: A man sued a haunted house attraction for being too scary and giving him nightmares.
verdict: Real — though the case was dismissed because the whole point is to be scared.

text: A couple sued a wedding photographer because the bride's eyes were closed in every single photo.
verdict: Fake — most photographers blink-correct before delivery.

text: A man sued his own parents for giving birth to him without his consent.
verdict: Real — he sought damages for being born. The court declined.

text: A man sued a casino after losing all his money, arguing the carpets were hypnotising him.
verdict: Fake — carpet hypnosis is not a recognised tort.

text: An astronaut sued NASA for refusing to let them keep a moon rock as a souvenir.
verdict: Fake — astronauts are not known to have sued over moon rocks.

text: A woman sued a lottery company because her winning numbers came true a week after the draw.
verdict: Fake — regret is not a cause of action.

text: A man sued an airline after he was seated next to an obese passenger who took up part of his seat.
verdict: Real — airlines have faced exactly this kind of suit.

text: A teenager sued their parents for naming them something embarrassing, claiming emotional distress.
verdict: Fake — although several countries restrict baby names, distress suits are not the route.

text: A man sued a pumpkin patch because the pumpkin he bought rotted before Halloween.
verdict: Real — small-claims suits over rotting pumpkins have happened.

text: A man sued a coffee shop because their 'small' was actually quite large.
verdict: Fake — generous portions have not been litigated as fraud.

text: A teacher sued a school after being fired for refusing to mark homework in red pen.
verdict: Fake — but some schools did once trial banning red pen.

text: A company was sued because its 'invisible' sunscreen was, in fact, visible.
verdict: Real — cosmetic claims have faced false-advertising suits.

text: A man sued a zoo because a gorilla made a face at his child and 'traumatised' the family.
verdict: Fake — gorilla expressions are not actionable.

text: A woman sued a TV show for spoiling the ending of a programme she had not yet watched.
verdict: Fake — there is no tort of spoiler liability.

text: A man sued his barber for giving him a haircut that made him look like a convicted criminal.
verdict: Fake — resemblance to a mugshot is not a recognised injury.

text: A couple sued a restaurant because the menu described the cake as 'heavenly' but it was merely nice.
verdict: Fake — menu poetry is protected by exaggeration.

text: A woman sued a dating app because every match she met turned out to be married.
verdict: Real — apps have been sued over misleading profiles.

text: A man sued his GPS navigation voice for being too cheerful and giving him road rage.
verdict: Fake — there is no claim against cheerful Sat Navs.

text: A class-action lawsuit was filed against a company for making crisps that were mostly air.
verdict: Real — snack-bag 'slack fill' has been the subject of real suits.

text: A man sued his mirror for reflecting an older version of himself than he felt inside.
verdict: Fake — mirrors have sovereign immunity.
